coredata.dc:title: Sensor array based on carbon dots for ATP-related physiological phosphates detecting and ATP hydrolysis monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shun
Li, Xiaona
Wang, Kai
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
Liu, Xue
coredata.dc:description: A simple indicator displacement assay (IDA)-based array system was established by using Fe(III)-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and Fe(III) to detect ATP-related physiological phosphates (PPs) and monitor ATP hydrolysis. The analysis was based on principal component analysis of differential CDs fluorescence regulated by Fe(III)/PPs complexes. By two modeling methods, two sensor arrays were established. That is, three CDs/one Fe(III) concentration (Array1) and one CD/four Fe(III) concentrations (Array2). Compared with Array1, Array2 saved a lot of trouble in preparing the CD sensors and the analytical performance it is hardly affected. The Array2 can distinguish PPs in serum environment and from interfering substances, such as amino acids and metal ions. Besides, quantitative analysis of ATP single-component system and qualitative analysis of ATP/ADP two-component system can be achieved. On this basis, ATP hydrolysis processes were monitored, and results suggested that ATP hydrolysis completed within 20 min under the catalysis of 24 U/mL apyrase. As ATP hydrolysis works for 30 min, its main products were mainly AMP and orthophosphate (Pi).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Physiological phosphates
Carbon dots
Sensor array
ATP hydrolysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of detecting ATP-related PPs and ATP hydrolysis processes with the IDA-based sensor array.

(a) TEM image, (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) XPS full-survey spectrum, (d) high-resolution C1 s spectrum, (e) high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum, (f) UV–Vis spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (λex = 380 nm) of the CD0.
(A) Fingerprints of PPs analyzed by the Array1, (B) PCA scattergraph of PPs generated by the Array1.
(A) Fingerprints of PPs analyzed by the Array2, (B) PCA scattergraph of PPs generated by the Array2.

(a) Fingerprints of PPs with serum analyzed by the Array2, (b) PCA scattergraph of PPs with serum generated by the Array2, (c) Fingerprints of PPs and interfering substances (including Cys, Gly, Try, Thr, NaAc, K2SO4, and CaCl2) analyzed by the Array2, (d) PCA scattergraph of PPs and interfering substances (including Cys, Gly, Try, Thr, NaAc, K2SO4, and CaCl2) generated by the Array2.

(a) Fingerprints of ATP with various concentrations analyzed by the Array2, (b) Up: PCA scattergraph of ATP with various concentrations generated by the Array2, Down: Linear calibration plot between PC1 and ATP concentrations from 1 μM to 50 μM, (c) Fingerprints of ATP/ADP binary mixtures analyzed by the Array2, (d) PCA scattergraph of ATP/ADP binary mixtures generated by the Array2.

(a) Fingerprints of ATP, ADP/Pi, AMP/2Pi, and hydrolysis products of ATP at regular intervals analyzed by the Array2, (b) PCA scattergraph of ATP, ADP/Pi, AMP/2Pi, and hydrolysis products of ATP at regular intervals generated by the Array2, (c) Linear calibration plot between PC1 and ATP hydrolysis time, (d) HCA dendrogram of ATP, ADP/Pi, AMP/2Pi, and hydrolysis products of ATP at regular intervals analyzed by the Array2.

head.ce:title: Sensor array based on carbon dots for ATP-related physiological phosphates detecting and ATP hydrolysis monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, (P. R. China)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple indicator displacement assay (IDA)-based array system was established by using Fe(III)-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and Fe(III) to detect ATP-related physiological phosphates (PPs) and monitor ATP hydrolysis. The analysis was based on principal component analysis of differential CDs fluorescence regulated by Fe(III)/PPs complexes. By two modeling methods, two sensor arrays were established. That is, three CDs/one Fe(III) concentration (Array1) and one CD/four Fe(III) concentrations (Array2). Compared with Array1, Array2 saved a lot of trouble in preparing the CD sensors and the analytical performance it is hardly affected. The Array2 can distinguish PPs in serum environment and from interfering substances, such as amino acids and metal ions. Besides, quantitative analysis of ATP single-component system and qualitative analysis of ATP/ADP two-component system can be achieved. On this basis, ATP hydrolysis processes were monitored, and results suggested that ATP hydrolysis completed within 20 min under the catalysis of 24 U/mL apyrase. As ATP hydrolysis works for 30 min, its main products were mainly AMP and orthophosphate (Pi).

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a nucleoside triphosphate used in cells to provide chemical energy to conduct a series of processes in biological systems [1–4]. Found in all forms of life, ATP is known as the "molecular unit of currency" of intracellular energy transfer [5,6]. When consumed in metabolic processes, ATP can be converted to various phosphate forms by enzymatic catalysis. ATP can be hydrolyzed to adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and orthophosphate (Pi) under ATPase catalysis, and ADP can be further hydrolyzed to adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and another Pi, which can supply energy for organism [7]. All of the relevant phosphates in ATP hydrolysis have important physiological significance [8]. For example, energy can be stored and released in living systems through protein phosphorylation and dephosphorylation in the regulation of metabolic processes. Phosphates are also the main buffering agent in animal cells and the structural material of bone and teeth after biomineralization [9,10]. The imbalance of ATP hydrolysis can change the burn rate and concentration of ATP, and then induce a series of diseases through affecting the coordination of energy supply and demand, including hypoglycemia, ischemia, hypoxia and Parkinson’s disease [11–13]. Therefore, it is highly essential to monitor the hydrolysis process of ATP and the levels of various forms of physiological phosphates (PPs).

Sensor array analysis is an important analytical approach to discriminate and classify a class of analytes with similar structures [14]. The analysis of a sensor array is realized through collecting signals generated from a group of sensors after their interaction with analytes [15–17]. Hence, sufficient sensing units should be applied and nonspecific interactions between sensors and analytes are necessary for the generation of cumulative responses [18–21]. In order to cut labor in design and synthesis of sensors, an indicator displacement assay (IDA) method is recently introduced in the construction of a sensor array. The IDA introduces a sensing medium, and makes use of the competition between the analyte and the sensor for the selective binding of sensing media to generate sensing signal [22,23]. The number of sensing units in the array system can be increased through the regulation of the species or concentrations of the sensing media, instead of the labor-consuming and time-costing preparation and modification of sensing units. There are some reports about applying IDA-based array systems for detecting phosphates by using fluorescent dyes [16], rare earth-based fluorescent nanomaterials [20], fluorescent polymers [21] and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). Among these sensing materials, CDs with simple synthesis method, excellent sensing performance and low toxicity are very suitable for the detection of PPs [24–27]. Despite the success of applying CD-based sensor arrays to discriminate phosphates, very few attempts have been made to analyze ATP-related PPs and monitor ATP enzymatic hydrolysis.

Herein, a simple IDA-based array system was designed by using Fe(III)-responsive CDs combined with various concentrations of Fe(III) to discriminate and detect ATP-related PPs. Fe(III) can combine with PPs and form coordination complexes through Fe-P bonds [28]. With the help of the specific interaction between Fe(III) and PPs, an effective IDA-based array system for PPs analysis can be constructed just by introducing a Fe(III)-responsive CD. In the IDA-based array system, Fe(III) acts as a bridge connecting PPs and CDs, and CDs act as the sensors to output sensing signals. The relay action from PPs to Fe(III), from Fe(III) to the CDs results in a notable fluorescence variation in the CDs. PPs with various structures will affect their actions with Fe(III), and stimulate a differential fluorescence response for array analysis. Different from common sensor array composed of multiple sensing units, the IDA-based array system took four concentrations of Fe(III) to improve their information richness and improve accuracy of the array analysis. The simply-designed array system performed an excellent analysis capability for PPs even in complicated physiological environments. With these merits, an efficient ATP hydrolysis monitoring system was designed based on the sensor array (Scheme 1 ).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was applied to observe the morphology of the CDs, which was carried out on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). The HR-TEM samples were prepared through dropping CD solution on a copper grid covered with a carbon film. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained on a Purkinje General TU-1900 (China). Photoluminescence (PL) was tested by RF-5301PC (SHIMADZU, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 5 and 5 nm, respectively. The finally present fluorescence spectra were processed by using normalized method through dividing each fluorescence intensity of the PL spectra by the maximum fluorescence intensity of the PL spectra.

Citric acid, adenosine 5-triphosphate disodium salt (ATP), adenosine 5-diphosphate sodium salt (ADP), adenosine 5-monophosphate sodium salt (AMP), pyrophosphate (PPi), sodium phosphate tribasic dodecahydrate (Pi), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dehydrate (NaH2PO4•2H2O), disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4•12H2O), L-alanine (Ala), L-cysteine (Cys), L-threonine (Thr) and L-tryptophan (Try), glycine (Gly) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (China). Formamide, acetone, and methanol were purchased from the Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc), potassium sulfate (K2SO4), calcium dichloride (CaCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), acetic acid (CH3COOH) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from BioInd biological Industries (Israel). All chemicals are of analytical grade.

1.0 g citric acid was dissolved in formamide, and the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Successively, the mixed solution was heated at 160 °C for 2 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Finally, yellow transparent liquid was generated. The solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48 h to remove the excess precursors and small molecules. The CDs (CD0) was obtained after freeze drying and maintained at 4 °C for further use. Other CDs (CD1, CD2, and CD3) were prepared through the microwave method according to our previous study [29,30].

In order to maintain unity of all assays and quantify the CDs, the absorbance of the CD solution was calibrated to 0.1 at their optimum excitation wavelength before use. The optimum excitation wavelength was the excitation wavelength where the fluorescence intensity of the CDs reaches their maximum value (λexmax-CD0 = 380 nm, λexmax-CD1 = 330 nm, λexmax-CD2 = 350 nm, λexmax-CD3 = 330 nm). 10 μL of Fe(III) solution and 10 μL of PPs with a calculated concentration were added into 3.0 mL of CD solution in order. The variation of fluorescence intensity at their maximum emission wavelength was followed. The maximum emission wavelength is the emission wavelength where the fluorescence intensity of the CDs reaches their maximum value in their PL spectra (λemmax-CD0 = 450 nm, λemmax-CD1 = 375 nm, λemmax-CD2 = 415 nm, λemmax-CD3 = 380 nm).

Array1 (3 CDs × 7 PPs × 5 trails) was constructed composed of three kinds of CDs (CD1, CD2 and CD3), a single concentration of Fe(III) (150 μM), seven kinds of PPs (100 μM) and five repeated trails. The fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength was collected before and after adding PPs (e.g. ATP, AMP, ADP, PPi, Pi, NaH2PO4·2H2O, and Na2HPO4·12H2O), and normalized by deducting the initial CD fluorescence (I0) and then dividing by I0. The normalized array data were processed by principle component analysis (PCA) through the soft of Statistical Product and Service Solutions (SPSS).

Array2 (4 concentrations × 7 PPs × 5 trails) was constructed composed of CD0, four concentrations of Fe(III) (50 μM, 150 μM, 250 μM, and 500 μM), seven kinds of PPs (100 μM) and five repeated trails. The construction of the sensor array was based on the competitive Fe(III)-binding effect between the CDs and PPs. PPs could be detected by using the CDs and Fe(III) through an on-off-on three-state fluorescence emission. The first step of adding Fe(III) can result in fluorescence quenching of the CDs because of electron or energy transfer during CD-Fe(III) complexes forming process. Given the intense coordination effect of Fe(III), CD-Fe(III) complexes can be disassembled with further addition of PPs, and thus the fluorescence of the CDs recovers. Various concentrations of Fe(III) can regulate the interaction between CDs and Fe(III), as well as Fe(III) and PPs. The concentration of Fe(III) can affect not only the fluorescence quenching extent of the CDs, but also the binding amount of PPs. The differential fluorescence signal response can be generated when various concentrations of Fe(III) are introduced into the sensing system. All the subsequent analysis processes including this were similar to Array1.

Two interference experiments were performed based on Array1 and Array2. The first one took place in a simulated serum environment. The final test solution contained 0.2 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 50 μM of PPs. The second one was designed considering the possible interfering substances in physiological analysis, including Cys, Gly, Try, Thr, NaAc, K2SO4, and CaCl2 [25]. The concentration of each interfering substance was set at 50 μM in the final test solution.

ATP concentration-related samples, ATP/ADP mixed samples and ATP hydrolysis samples were analyzed by Array2. In the ATP concentration-related experiments, five concentrations (1 μM, 6 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM and 50 μM) of ATP were prepared in the test samples. In assay experiments for the ATP/ADP mixed samples, the total concentration of PPs in the test samples was 200 μM, and seven kinds of molar ratios (8/2, 7/3, 6/4, 5/5, 4/6, 3/7, and 2/8) of ATP/ADP binary mixtures were prepared and analyzed combined with single ADP and ATP samples.

ATP can be hydrolyzed into ADP, AMP and Pi in the presence of apyrase. ATP hydrolytic experiment was carried out in 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) containing 4.0 mM EDTA, 15.0 mM NaCl and 0.35 mM KCl at 37 °C [31]. The hydrolytic experiment was catalyzed by apyrase composed of ATPase and ADPase (ATPase/ADPase = 1/1). The initial samples contained 50 mM ATP and 24 U/mL apyrase, and the hydrolysis samples were gathered and tested at different intervals (0, 10, 20 and 30 min).

Principal components analysis (PCA) is a common technique to process data sets through simplifying the dimensions of data sets without losing critical information [24,32]. PCA can convert the obtained data array into a new set of linear uncorrelated principal components (PCs) [24]. PCA factor scores, serving as important parameters to evaluate the contribution of each individual sensing unit to the construction of PCs, have been demonstrated in the supporting information. The cumulative proportions of PCs exceed 90 % can provide an adequate information for PCA analysis, and hence only PC1 and PC2 were listed and applied for the following analysis. The correlation formulas for PC1 and PC2 were obtained from the PCA factor scores, and applied to plot PCA scattergraphs by taking PC1 and PC2 as horizontal and vertical coordinates and realize the data visualization.

CD1, CD2 and CD3 were prepared referring to our previous work [29,30], and hence no more characterizations they were shown here. This paper mainly characterized the morphology, composition, and photochemical properties of the freshly prepared CD0 by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The TEM image illustrated that the CD0 were well-dispersed in water, with average particle sizes of about 3.1 nm (Fig. 1 a). The FT-IR and XPS results can reveal the characteristic functional groups on the CD0. There exist obvious stretching vibrations of CN/C=O, C=C, and C–N (1380-1613 cm−1), the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C-O–C (1068 and 1185 cm−1), stretching vibrations of methyl/methylene C-H (2850 and 2917 cm−1) in the FT-IR spectrum of the CD0 (Fig. 1b). Besides, the broad absorption bands from 3200−3430 cm−1 indicate the presence of amino (–NH2) and hydroxyl (OH) functional groups on the surface of the CD0. From XPS analysis of the CD0 (Fig. 1c–e), it can be seen that the CD0 mainly contains C, O, and N elements. The high-resolution XPS C1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into five binding energies (C-C/CC: 284.4 eV, CN: 285.3 eV, C-O: 286.3 eV, CN=/C = O: 287.7 eV, and COOH: 288.5 eV) (Fig. 1d) [33]. In the high-resolution XPS N1 s spectrum, two fitting peaks at 399.2 and 401.1 eV are corresponding to the graphitic-like/amino N and pyrrolic-like N, respectively (Fig. 1e) [34,35]. Two fitting peaks of O 1s XPS at 531.6 and 532.9 eV are attributed to C=O and C-O bonds (Figure S1, Supporting Information). These characterizations show the CD0 can be considered to be composed of nanoscaled graphite-like skeleton/core, and covered with N and O containing heterocyclic chemical structures and abundant amino and hydroxyl functional groups [34]. This is consistent with the observation from those from CD1, CD2 and CD3, demonstrating that these four CDs have similar carbon skeleton structures and minor differences in surficial functional groups. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CD0 exhibits two absorption peaks, focused at 239 and 344 nm (Fig. 1f), corresponding to the π–π* transition of CN bonds and the n–π* transitions of CO bonds or their neighboring groups, respectively. Under the optimum excitation wavelength (380 nm), the dilute solution of CD0 displays strong blue fluorescence (450 nm) with the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of 6.62 %.

The effects of NaCl concentrations, H2O2 concentrations, pH and storage time on CD0 were investigated to research the stability of CD0. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CD0 and the concentration of NaCl was shown in Figure S2, which illustrating the fluorescence intensity of CD0 was hardly affected by the ionic strength. With a sharp increase of H2O2 concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of CD0 decreased only a little. Within a lower concentration range (0–100 mM), the fluorescence intensity of CD0 did not change obviously, which indicating CD0 have stronger oxidation resistance. As shown in Figure S3, CD0 has good stability in a wide pH range (pH 3–10). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CD0 hardly changed with the increase of storage time. All of these results confirmed the superior stability of CD0. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of CD0 was researched. The fitted decay curve of CD0 sample was found to be mono-exponential with lifetimes of 9.63 ns (Figure S4).

In order to examine the feasibility of Fe(III)-regulated CD-array for PP analysis, Array1 was firstly applied to analyze a series of PPs (e.g. ATP, AMP, ADP, PPi, Pi, NaH2PO4·2H2O, and Na2HPO4·12H2O). Array1 is a typical sensor array including three sensors, and the sensing is achieved through a typical IDA mechanism. All of the CDs show a notable response to Fe(III) and a negligible response to PPs (Figure S5, S6). It can be seen from Fig. 2 a, Array1 exhibits unique fluorescence fingerprints to seven PPs. In the corresponding PCA scattergraph (Fig. 2b), PC1 and PC2 accounts for 85.17 % and 13.23 % of the total variability in the data respectively, and the total contributions of PC1 and PC2 exceed 90 % (Table S1), demonstrating that PC1 and PC2 contain most of the original information in the sensor array. Hence, PC1 and PC2 can be used to plot a PCA scattergraph as the horizontal and vertical coordinates respectively. The correlation formulas of PC1 and PC2 are listed in Table S2, and fluorescence variations were substituted in the formulas to plot corresponding data points in the PCA scattergraph. From the PCA scattergraph, all the data points from five repeated trials for each PP are close to each other and can be marked with a circle, representing an exclusive zone for a specific PP. Seven PPs are separated without any overlap with each other. These results demonstrate that the Array1 can detect and discriminate PPs as designed.

In order to further prove the anti-interference capability of Array1, two kinds of interference environment were choose to tested the influence of serum and other substances on Array1. Seven PPs with the addition of the serum can be distinguished from each other. And these PPs can also be separated from these interfering substances, indicating the Array1 with a high selectivity and anti-interference capability for PPs (Figure S8).

Investigating the feasibility of applying Fe(III)-responsive CDs to construct a sensor array and realize the detection and identification of PPs, a simplified array was constructed as Array2 by taking only one sensor. Due to a reduced sensor number, the construction of Array2 is more convenient with less synthetic work compared with Array1. Without multiple sensors, the array analysis of Array2 is achieved through using different concentrations of Fe(III) to regulate the status of surficial functional groups of the single sensor and form various evolving sensors. Four concentrations of Fe(III) (including 50 μM, 150 μM, 250 μM, and 500 μM) were chosen from the highly sensitive range of the CD0 to Fe(III) in order to obtain a highly sensitive responding result for PPs (Fig. 3 ). And compared with 0.8–1.45 mM of phosphate contained in human serum, Array2 can discriminate ATP, ADP, AMP, Pi and PPi at a relatively low concentration of 100 μM [36,37]. From the PCA scattergraph of PPs generated by Array2, no evident intersection among the data groups from various PPs was observed, indicating that Array2 also shows superior analytical performance for PPs under the same concentration level of PPs to Array1. The high cross-reactivity given by CD-Fe(III)-PPs system can be attributed to efficient regulation of various concentrations of Fe(III) to the surficial compositions and photochemical properties of the CD0.

ATP hydrolysis is the major method the human body uses to chemically release energy. Accompanied by energy release, a series of PPs are transferred and related to each other in physiological environments. Therefore, monitoring ATP hydrolysis-related PPs in physiological environments can evaluate the metabolic level of ATP in vivo and further assess health levels. Serum is part of the most important physiological environment, and applied in here as a background interference additive to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the Array2 for practical samples. Though the serum itself possesses a unique fluorescence response when processed by the Array2, seven PPs with the addition of the serum can be distinguished from each other by using the Array2 (Fig. 4 a-b). Such results clearly demonstrate the high specificity and discriminative capability of the Array2 for PPs. Amino acids and salts are also common interfering substances in physiological analysis, which was added to further evaluate the selectivity and specificity of the Array2. Interestingly, these PPs can also be separated from these interfering substances (including Cys, Gly, Try, Thr, NaAc, K2SO4, and CaCl2), implying the Array2 with a high selectivity for PPs (Fig. 4c-d). Both these two results clearly demonstrate that Array2 is of high anti-interference capability.

A series of practical applications were carried out based on the analysis results obtained from the Array2. Firstly, Array2 was applied to monitor the concentration of ATP. Various concentrations of ATP ranging from 1 μM to 50 μM were processed by the Array2, and their fingerprint is given in Fig. 5 a. Interestingly, the data points from the same ATP concentrations not only gather together and are separated from those from the other concentrations, but they also present an obvious uniform distribution trend from left to right with the increase of ATP concentrations (Fig. 5b-up). Owing to the PC1 in the array analysis greater than 95 %, it is acceptable to use the single PC1 to describe the pattern of ATP with different concentrations. There exists a good linear relationship between PC1 and ATP concentrations (Fig. 5b-down), and the fitting line can be utilized to quantify ATP in applications. Secondly, a series of ATP/ADP binary mixtures were identified by the Array2, including 10/0, 8/2, 7/3, 6/4, 5/5, 4/6, 3/7, 2/8, 0/10 (molar ratios). Mitochondrial respiration needs to be accomplished with the help of cytochrome c oxidase (CytOx) catalysis, and the activity of CytOx is influenced by not only ATP but also ATP/ADP ratios [38]. As shown in Fig. 5c-d, Array2 displays a clear discriminative performance for these groups of ATP/ADP binary mixtures. The data points from the mixed ATP/ADP samples are located between those from pure ATP and ADP. Relatively speaking, the data points from the mixtures with a high content of ADP (samples: 2/8 and 3/7) are close to those from ADP and lie in the left half of the PCA scattergraph. Therefore, a possible ATP/ADP ratio can be forecasted based on the location of the data points in the PCA scattergraph of PPs with the help of simple array analysis by Array2.

The proposed Array2 was applied to monitor ATP hydrolysis because of its excellent identification capacity for various PPs. ATP can be hydrolyzed into ADP and Pi in the presence of ATPase and finally be in the form of AMP + 2 Pi under the catalysis of ADPase. In order to simulate the ATP hydrolysis in a practical environment as far as possible, the hydrolytic enzyme (apyrase) is a mixture enzyme including ATPase and ADPase, and the hydrolysis is accomplished under the optimal conditions (such as biomimetic buffer and suitable temperature). ATP samples, ADP/Pi samples, and AMP/2Pi samples with the same PPs concentrations were modeled after the array analysis by Array2 (Fig. 6 a-b). Hydrolysis samples were taken at regular intervals, processed by the formula formed by pre-modeling, and plotted in the PCA scattergraph. From the PCA scattergraph of pre-modeling by ATP, ADP/Pi and AMP/2Pi, it can be speculated that the data points might obey a certain distribution trend from right to left as ATP hydrolysis proceeds. Interestingly, all of the experimental results just comply with the expectation. The data points from the initiation site (0 min) nearly fall into the zone of ATP samples. The hydrolysis gradually pushes the data points moving from right to left. The data points within 20 min are mainly in the first stage of hydrolysis dominated by ATPase, and the second stage of hydrolysis by ADPase may start after 20 min. Similar to ATP concentration analysis experiments with a PC1 higher than 90 %, the PC1 here can also be applied to analyze the hydrolysis process. A good linear calibration plot between PC1 and ATP hydrolysis time is shown (Fig. 6c), which can give a more direct, simple and visual way to follow the hydrolysis process compared with PCA scattergraph. For example, data points at 10 min are situated in the interval between ATP and ADP/Pi, indicating that the initial hydrolysis stage from ATP to ADP and Pi proceeds at this moment. Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) is a way of cluster analysis, which seeks to build a hierarchy of clusters and realize data mining and statistics similar to PCA. HCA is applied here to give a more distinct and accurate account for the hydrolysis process (Fig. 6d). At 10 and 20 min, there exist mainly ADP and Pi in solution, indicating that the first hydrolysis stage catalysed mainly by ATPase is in progress then. It can be noted that the solution composition at 30 min turns into AMP and Pi when ADPase hydrolyzes ADP. All of these conclusions from various analytical methods can coincide with each other and confirm the superior performance of the Array2 for ATP hydrolysis monitoring.

In summary, we designed an IDA-based sensor array of ATP-related PPs detecting and ATP hydrolysis monitoring. The sensing system was constructed by using a Fe(III)-responsive CD combined with Fe(III). The sensor array can discriminate ATP, ADP, AMP, Pi and PPi at a relatively low concentration of 100 μM, even in complicated test environment with serum, amino acids or metal ions. An ATP concentration-related fitting line was generated by the sensor array and can be used to quantify ATP in the concentration range of 1–50 μM. The sensor array can also be used to follow the ratio of ATP/ADP, an important physiological variable. ATP hydrolysis was further followed at different intervals. The sensor array can accurately anticipate the hydrolysis process of ATP, including hydrolysis time, hydrolysis products and hydrolysis mechanism. This system could potentially be applied in disease diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873085), Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province of China (20180510023 and 20180550947), Program for the Top Young and Middle-aged Innovative Talents of Higher Learning Institutions of Liaoning Province of China (LR2018010), Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (LJC201906) and Shenyang Municipal Program for the Top Young and Middle-aged Innovative Talents of Liaoning Province of China (RC170258).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127851.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Artificial receptors for the recognition of phosphorylated molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. E. Hargrove, S. Nieto, T. Zhang, J. L. Sessler and E. V. Anslyn, Artificial receptors for the recognition of phosphorylated molecules, Chem. Rev. 111 (2011) 6603-6782.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First supramolecular sensors for phosphonate anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. A. Esipenko, P. Koutnik, T. Minami, First supramolecular sensors for phosphonate anions, Chem. Sci. 4 (2013) 3617-3623.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of a fluorescence-turn-on sensor array for phosphates in blood serum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. V. Zyryanov, M. A. Palacios, P. Anzenbacher, Rational design of a fluorescence-turn-on sensor array for phosphates in blood serum, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 46 (2007) 7849-7852.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ATP is a mediator of chemosensory transduction in the central nervous system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. V. Gourine, E. Llaudet, N. Dale and K. M. Spyer, ATP is a mediator of chemosensory transduction in the central nervous system, Nature, 436 (2005) 108-111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Azulene-based macrocyclic receptors for recognition and sensing of phosphate anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. H. Sheng, L. Shao, J. J. Chen, W. J. Bao, F. B. Wang, and X. H. Xia, Catalyst-free synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene via thermal annealing graphite oxide with melamine and its excellent electrocatalysis, ACS nano. 5 (2011) 4350-4358.
R. F. Schmidt, G. Thews, Human physiology, Springer, 1989.
W. J. Marshall, S. K. Bangert, Clinical chemistry, Elsevier, Edinburg, 2004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase is inhibited by ATP only at very high ATP/ADP ratios

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Ramzan, A. K. Schaper, P. Weber, A. Rhiel, M. S. Siddiq, S. Vogt, Mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase is inhibited by ATP only at very high ATP/ADP ratios, Biol. Chem. 398 (2017) 737-750.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shun Hu is working for her MS degree from the Department of chemistry, majoring in Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon nanomaterials and research on its related applications.
Xiaona Li is working for her MS degree from the Department of chemistry, majoring in chemical engineering, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon nanomaterials.
Kai Wang graduated from master's degree in the Department of chemistry, majoring in Chemical engineering, Liaoning University. His work has been the applications of carbon nanomaterials.

Qiuhua Wu is an associate professor in College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. She received hPhD degree from College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China in 2016. Now, she is engaged in the simulation of the biomineralization.

Guolin Zhang is a professor at College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received his PhD degree from College of Chemistry, Nankai University, China in 2004. Now, he is engaged in Janus materials, carbon nanomaterials and sensors.

Xue Liu is an associate professor at the College of chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received his PhD degree from the college of chemistry, NanKai University, China in 2013. Now, he is engaged in the preparation and application of the carbon nanomaterials.
